mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 2 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW 2 Series engine typically costs £2,200–£6,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], with the volume B47 diesel at £2,800–£4,500 and the B58 petrol at £3,800–£6,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 2 Series engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30 straight-six — the most dependable modern BMW engine in our dataset. The B47D20A diesel and B48B20 petrol are also excellent. Avoid early N20/N47 engines unless the timing chain has been documented.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 2 Series engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a pre-2016 N20B20 or N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engines themselves are solid; the timing chain tensioner design is the problem. A 2016+ B47/B48/B58 has no such caveat.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my 2 Series timing chain is worn?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning, sometimes with a chain-noise change under load. On the N20, listen for a high-pitched whine at startup. On the N47, the rattle is from the rear of the engine. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 2 Series engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on bodystyle and generation. Against a £25,000 M240i, a £5,000 B58 recon is just 20% of the car's value — ✅ safe. Against a £7,000 Active Tourer, a £3,500 B47 recon is 50% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £6,000 early F22 N47, a £4,500 recon is 75% — ❌ walk away or run the maths carefully. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 2 Series should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the sports car experience: 2017+ F22 LCI M240i with the B58 — RWD, 340 bhp, and depreciated. For the family car: any F45/F46 Active/Gran Tourer with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol — the most practical BMW you can buy. For style: F44 Gran Coupé with M235i xDrive. Avoid pre-2016 N20/N47 cars without documented chain history.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 2 Series engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your bodystyle, model year and variant (218i, 220d, M240i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: BMW 2 Series vs 3 Series — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N20/N47/B48/B58 story is identical. The 2 Series is lighter (especially the F22), so it works the powertrain less hard; the 3 Series adds weight but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge.

hero.h1: 2 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW 2 Series engine across five distinct bodystyles — from the B38 three-cylinder in the Active Tourer to the B58 straight-six in the M240i. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for every F22 to G42. Backed by 400+ real UK 2 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The 2 Series rule — bodystyle is destiny: a B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £25,000 M240i is 15–24% of the car's value (✅ safe); the same engine in a high-mileage F22 at £10,000 is 38–60% (⚠️ watch). Run the maths per car, not per badge.
★★★★☆ — the B-family engines (B38, B47, B48, B58) are the most dependable; the N20 and N47 carry the chain-tensioner caveat on early cars.
B58B30 (M240i/M135i xDrive) and post-2016 B47D20A / B48B20
Pre-2016 N47D20C and N20B20 without documented timing-chain replacement — affects early F22/F23 diesels and 228i petrols
2017+ F22 LCI with B58 (M240i) — the modern straight-six in a compact RWD chassis, depreciation already baked in
F45/F46 Active/Gran Tourer with B47D20A diesel — the sensible family MPV with BMW reliability
🏆 Most Diverse BMW Model — 5 bodystyles, 2 platforms, 12+ engines

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The 2 Series is BMW's most diverse family — a RWD sports coupé, a FWD family MPV, and a four-door coupé all wearing the same badge. The engines are mostly excellent; the bodystyle you choose matters more than the engine you pick.

H2: BMW 2 Series — One Badge, Five Distinct Cars

rows.metric: Sub-headline: The 2 Series is BMW's most diverse family. Your ownership experience depends entirely on which bodystyle and which platform you choose — they share a badge but little else.
BMW 2 Series Coupé/Convertible F22/F23 🔥 Most Enquired — 147 enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The spiritual successor to the E30 — RWD, compact, and available with the B58 straight-six in the M240i. Early N20/N47 cars carry the chain caveat; 2017+ LCI cars with B48/B58 are the sweet spot. The M240i is a future classic.

rows.metric: Our Verdict: The second-generation RWD coupé — CLAR platform, B-family engines only, and the M240i xDrive with 374 bhp. No manual gearbox, but the B58 is the most dependable modern BMW engine. The safest 2 Series ever made.
Our Verdict: BMW's first front-wheel-drive production car — a compact MPV that competes with the Mercedes B-Class. The B47 diesel is the sensible choice; the 225xe PHEV is the urban commuter's pick.

rows.value: Our Verdict: The seven-seat Active Tourer — the most practical 2 Series you can buy. Same B47/B48 engines, more space. The sensible family hauler that still wears a BMW badge.

rows.metric: Our Verdict: The four-door coupé on the UKL2 platform — BMW's answer to the Mercedes CLA. Shares its engines with the 1 Series F40. The M235i xDrive with 306 bhp is the pick; the 218i with B38 is the entry point.
The RWD vs FWD Divide — What You Need to Know

rows.value: Read the full comparison: BMW 2 Series RWD vs FWD →

rows.metric: The Badge Ambiguity — "220i" Means Different Things
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The "220i" badge covers three different engines depending on bodystyle and year. On the F22 (2014–2016), it's the N20B20 (2.0L turbo, 184 bhp) — the chain-tensioner engine. On the F22 LCI (2017+), it's the B48B20 (2.0L, 184 bhp) — the revised architecture. On the F44 Gran Coupé (2020+), it's also the B48B20, but on the UKL2 FWD platform. All share a badge. None share a reliability profile or driving character. Ensure the model pages call out the distinction.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 2 Series Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW 2 Series — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

engines.engineCode: — (pattern data — under B58 aggregate in source data)

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: B58B30 and B47D20A ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume modern engines with clear failure patterns and shared-platform data from the 3 Series and 1 Series. N47D20C and N20B20 ratings: 85% confidence — well-documented timing chain issues from BMW TSBs and specialist reports. B38A15 rating: Expert-knowledge tier — the three-cylinder is relatively young and low-volume in our dataset, but shared with Mini and the 1 Series F40. N55B30 rating: Pattern-data tier — documented as a robust engine in the M235i. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The source data lists "2 Series" as a single entry, but the 2 Series is five distinct bodystyles across two platforms. The F22 (147 enquiries) and F45 Active Tourer (123 enquiries) are the only model-level counts in the dataset. Other variants (F23, F44, F46, G42) are aggregated or pattern-data-tier until the source file can separate them. Correction applied: The N20B20 timing chain issue affects early F22 228i models (2014–2016). This is the petrol equivalent of the N47 diesel chain problem — both were resolved with the B-series engine replacements. Correction applied: The B38A15 (1.5L three-cylinder) is fitted to the 218i Active Tourer and 218i Gran Coupé. It is not a "budget" engine — it's surprisingly refined and economical. Correction applied: The F22 M235i (2014–2016) used the N55B30, while the F22 LCI M240i (2017+) uses the B58B30. The two share a power output badge but are mechanically distinct — the B58 is significantly more reliable.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the 2 Series family — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining early 2 Series issue — affects 2014–2016 F22/F23 with N20 petrol and N47 diesel engines. Cold-start rattle is the warning; chain slip destroys the engine. £2,800–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].

The B-family engines' known weak points — coolant expansion tank leaks and PCV valve failures. Affects F45/F46 Active/Gran Tourer and F44 Gran Coupé. Repairs are £400–£800, not catastrophic if caught early.

The B47 diesel's primary weak points — EGR cooler leaks and DPF clogging on urban-driven cars. Affects all B47-powered 2 Series (2015+). Well-documented, repairable, far less severe than the N47's chain issue.

The Active Tourer and Gran Tourer are often used for school runs and city driving — exactly the conditions that kill diesel particulate filters. Check DPF health before buying any urban-used diesel 2 Series MPV.
The early M235i's Achilles heel — high-pressure fuel pump and injector issues. Affects F22 M235i (2014–2016). Check history; BMW extended warranty covered many.
The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and plastic fitting failures. Affects F22 LCI M240i and G42 M240i xDrive. £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW 2 Series Market Intelligence — What 400+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the M240i is a future classic [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

signals.signal: BMW 2 Series F45 Active Tourer enquiries
signals.demandTrend: ➡ Stable — family MPV demand is steady [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

signals.signal: BMW 2 Series F44 Gran Coupé enquiries
signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — newer model entering warranty-exit age [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The 2 Series is BMW's most diverse model line — but the enquiry data shows a split personality. The F22 Coupé (147 enquiries) generates interest from performance buyers researching M240i engine options. The F45 Active Tourer (123 enquiries) generates enquiries from families facing DPF and EGR issues. Two very different customers, one badge. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

The F22's N20/N47 timing chain issue continues to drive enquiries years after production ended — exactly the same pattern as the 3 Series E90/F30 N47 question. Early 2 Series buyers are doing the same due diligence: "has the chain been done?" (Editorial synthesis.)

Petrol enquiries (N55, B58) are concentrated on the M235i/M240i — performance buyers researching upgrades or repairs, not families responding to failures. The B58's 540 enquiries (JLR-wide) are largely from higher-value cars where owners are doing due diligence. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 2 Series Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one 2 Series based on this page, decide which 2 Series you actually want first — a RWD sports coupé, a FWD family MPV, or a four-door coupé. Then buy the newest B-family engine you can afford. The B58 in the M240i is the modern gold standard; the B47 in the Active Tourer is the sensible family choice. But on an early F22 with N20 or N47, a documented timing-chain inspection is worth more than any service stamp.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW 2 Series Engine Replacement Costs — By Bodystyle
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 2 Series engine typically takes 8–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The 2 Series rule — bodystyle is destiny: A £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon against a £25,000 M240i sits at 15–24% of the car's value — ✅ safe. The same engine against a £10,000 early F22 is 38–60% — ⚠️ watch. And a £2,200–£3,800 B38 recon against a £7,000 Active Tourer is 31–54% — still safe, but run the maths. Value the car first, then the engine. On a pre-2016 N20/N47 with no chain history, budget for the job or walk away.

Value the car first, then the engine. On a pre-2016 N20/N47 with no chain history, budget for the job or walk away.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW 2 Series Engine Range Evolved — 2014 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW launched the 2 Series Coupé as the spiritual successor to the E30 3 Series. The N20 and N47 were carryover engines from the 1 Series/3 Series.
eras.worthKnowing: The N20/N47 timing chain issue defined this era — every early 2 Series diesel and 228i petrol lives in its shadow.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's UKL platform brought front-wheel-drive to the 2 Series badge. The B-family engines arrived with the Active Tourer — and later the Gran Tourer and Gran Coupé.
eras.worthKnowing: The B47 finally fixed the chain issue that defined the N47. The B38 three-cylinder is surprisingly refined.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The F22 LCI replaced the N20 and N47 with the B48 and B58 — the reliability story changed more than the styling. M235i became M240i.
eras.worthKnowing: The B58 M240i is a future classic — 340 bhp, RWD, and the most dependable modern BMW straight-six.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The CLAR-platform G42 arrived — BMW's last RWD coupé before the switch to electric. B-family engines only, no N-series carryover.
eras.worthKnowing: The G42 M240i xDrive with 374 bhp is the most powerful non-M 2 Series ever. No manual gearbox, but the B58 is bulletproof.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW 2 Series?

profiles.verdict: The F45 Active Tourer or F46 Gran Tourer are the practical choices — the Gran Tourer offers seven seats, the Active Tourer is a spacious five-seater. B47 diesel is the sensible pick; B38 petrol for urban use. Not a sports car, but a BMW family car.

The Active Tourer with B47 diesel or the Gran Coupé with B48 petrol are perfect commuter cars — comfortable, efficient, and modern. The F22 Coupé is less practical but more fun. Choose your bodystyle based on your commute.

A cheap early F22 N20/N47 is not a cheap 2 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 F22 LCI with B48/B58 is the bargain; a £8,000 pre-LCI without chain paperwork is the trap.

The M240i with the B58 is one of the greatest RWD coupés ever sold in Britain — 340 bhp in the F22 LCI, 374 bhp in the G42. The F22 M240i is a future classic; the G42 M240i xDrive is the modern performance king. Buy the F22 while values are still sensible.

The F44 Gran Coupé is the most stylish 2 Series — a four-door coupé with frameless doors and a sloping roofline. The M235i xDrive with the B48 is the performance pick; the 218i with B38 is the entry point. Buy the newest you can afford.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My BMW 2 Series Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Cold-start rattle on an N20/N47? Chain noise on an early F22? Or DPF issues on your Active Tourer? On the 2 Series, the answer depends on which bodystyle and which engine you have. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.
